summary: I was born on Daebudo, an island off the west coast of South Korea. This place holds deep memories of my father's generation and the hardships they endured during the country's turbulent times. After a 25-year separation, I returned to Daebudo on the eve of the 50th anniversary of the armistice of the Korean War to reconnect with my roots and honor those who sacrificed for our future. Standing among the chestnut trees my father planted, I felt a profound connection to this land and to him, even though I now live far away.
cues: island off the west coast of South Korea

summary: South Korea is where my story begins, born in Daebudo and shaped by the history of my family and country. The struggles and division caused by the Korean War deeply affected my father's generation and scattered my family across different places. Returning to South Korea after many years, I witnessed the changes and paid respects to those who endured so much hardship. This country is the foundation of my identity, even as I have grown and lived elsewhere.

My family moved to Minnesota after leaving South Korea, and it became my new home where I built my life. Minnesota represents the new world where I grew stronger and more deeply rooted, much like the chestnut trees my father planted back home. It is here that I have worked as an English teacher and technology integration specialist, shaping young minds for nearly two decades. Though far from South Korea, Minnesota is where I have created lasting memories and connections.

I have taught at Central High School in St. Paul, Minnesota for 19 years, serving as both an English teacher and a technology integration TOSA. St. Paul is where I have dedicated much of my professional life, helping students navigate their education and embrace new technologies. This city has been my base while I reflect on my heritage and the journey from South Korea to the United States. It is a place of growth and opportunity that contrasts with my birthplace but is equally important to who I am.

The Korean War profoundly shaped my family's history and the country I was born in. My father's generation lived through the suffering and trauma of a nation torn apart by conflict, which scattered my family across the world. The war's legacy is something I carry with me, especially as I returned to Daebudo on the eve of the 50th anniversary of the armistice to honor those who sacrificed their lives. The memories of this war are deeply intertwined with my identity and the story of my family.

I returned to Daebudo on the eve of the 50th anniversary of the Armistice of the Korean War, a moment filled with reflection and respect. This armistice marked a significant pause in the conflict that had torn my homeland apart and shaped my family's journey. Being there at that time allowed me to observe the changes in my birthplace and pay tribute to those who died so I could live the life I have today. The armistice is a powerful symbol of both loss and hope for my family and country.
